speaker: interviewer
text: Today is September 18, 1996. We're with Sally Muschel. I'm Jackie Lew. The interview is taking place in Brooklyn, New York, USA. And it's conducted in English. Today is September 18, 1996. We're with survivor Sally Muschel. I'm Jackie Lew. The interview is taking place in Brooklyn, New York, USA. And it's conducted in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell me your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sally Muschel. At home, I was called Salusha.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How do you spell your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-A-L-L-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-U-S-C-H-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And the name you were born with?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Sala Michlowitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-I-C-H-L-O-W-I-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your first name was Sally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sally, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what name were you called, you just said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Salusha.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brindl [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name was Muschel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And spell that for me, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: M-U-S-C-H-E-L. It's the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Yosef Michlowitz, M-I-C-H-L-O-W-I-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born Dombrowa [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: D-O-M-B-R-O-W-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Galicia--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: October 22, 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm now gonna be 67.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had a brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Simcha. And there was a big accident, 1937. He fell in-- he was burned with hot water. And he died. He was three years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And who lived in the house with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our house lived-- my grandmother lived with us. And then in the same house lived my aunt with my cousin, which she was a widow. And they ate with us together. They had their own apartment, but they ate with us together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what kind of house did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a big house. We had a tenant. We-- there were a few stores downstairs. And there was our apartment. There was my aunt's apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family own the building?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What business did your father have?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] concession from a brewery, from beer. And he bottled it himself. And we also had concession from liqueur.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they considered upper class or middle class?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were considered upper class, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your childhood? What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what I remember, I went to public school, and I also learned Hebrew. I-- I was very spoiled. I was one child after my brother died, especially. And I had a very, very happy childhood. We used to go away summer and winter. And I was really-- but my cousin was-- which was a few years older than I was, but she was to me like sister. And I was extremely happy till the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And before that, tell me about the holidays.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, our holidays, we had-- first thing every Shabbat, my father used to go from one shul to another, and used to pick up poor people. And I remember this one incident is very much in my mind. He brought in people which looked really, really terrible, you know? They were without clothing, without a-- a matter of fact, I remember-- this was just before the war. The-- our-- we had the Jewish cook, which she refused to-- to serve and to sit at the table. And after this, I ask-- I told my father-- I said, listen. My friends also bring some people, but they don't-- they didn't bring such bad-- such homeless people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father says, you see, you said that this friend brings-- [NON-ENGLISH] Rosenthal, and this one brings people home. And where shall they go? Should they remain without food? And then usually Shabbat at night, my-- they used to-- my father used to tell them to come. And he gave them clothing, gave them money. And one time-- this was-- this was the same time-- they left. So I said, you see? They didn't even say thank you. So my father says, listen. I want you to know one thing-- God created the world, and he gave everybody equal amount of money. For some reason, he gave some other people somebody else's money. What I give them, this is theirs, no mine. I have to pray to God I shouldn't spend more than is only this, what is mine. And this remains with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I mean, this was-- I-- I think that this was already soon-- maybe '38 or '39, 'cause I remembered it very clearly. I had a lot of friends. I had Jewish friends. I also had non-Jewish friends. I had a lot of non-Jewish friends. I was close with them and we had a very good relationship. And I didn't feel the anti-Semitism except the once, which I also remember. One of the girls brought a-- a toy to school. And she said, the Jewish children cannot see this, but you, Sally, come with-- with me. And I told her I'm also Jewish and I don't want to go. This was about the one episode of anti-Semitism which I felt in our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at this time, I probably was seven, because this one at school just as [INAUDIBLE] before the war. And I never wanted to be a friend with her anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your family's religious background.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were very religious, very Orthodox. My grandfather came from directly [INAUDIBLE] order. My grandfather, I don't remember. This is my maternal grandparents. And they were-- there was a extremely Orthodox home. But, you know-- but there was-- education was very stressed. I remember my father taught me Chumash. And when I knew it by heart, he took me to Talmud Torah, because he used to go to-- to the Talmud Torah because usually, the children went to public school-- in the afternoon, they went to the Talmud Torah. And he took me to the boys' Talmud Torah-- I should say it by heart, [NON-ENGLISH]. I still remember it. And I still remember it by heart.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he taught me a lot. And I went to a school, which I learned Hebrew. And that's-- And how did you celebrate the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we celebrate all the holidays. My aunt was with us. My grandmother was with us. And sometimes our neighbor-- the daughter. Their daughter used to come in because she used to like very much Passover. And we all saw the maid, which had celebrated Christmas. And she had a tree in our house. [LAUGHS] And I remember I was very happy to decorate the tree.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your town look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very small town. It was the square, which was the [NON-ENGLISH]. And there were, like, few streets-- even though considered a [POLISH] town, which means that there was-- the court was there. There were two schools and-- but it was a very tiny town. At this time, it didn't look to me so tiny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there a lot of Jewish families there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were a lot of Jewish families. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember your family talking about the anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too much. But I remembered episode, which was in 1939, just before the war. And the city, like, mayor, which was [NON-ENGLISH] called my father in. And-- and my father took me along. And he was very nice. He gave us tea. And then he asked for-- for money for bonds-- like here, bonds. And they're called-- and-- [POLISH]. And he said to him, but you have to give me this and this amount. And my father says, but this is a tremendous amount. I can't afford. So he says to them, Mr. Michlowitz, I am asking you, but you must give. And then I remember my father said then he's a big anti-Semite. But as a rule, I didn't really feel too much anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your family having to go speak to the rabbi or-- or anybody in the Jewish community about the problems?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, the Rabbi Weidenfeld was a close friend of our family. We were friends with his grandchildren. My mother was friends with his children. We were extremely close family. We were always going there. I remember at one point, I called him Zaide Rabbi Weidenfeld. He was exceptional person. I remember him very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I know that my father learned a lot and he respected-- this I all find out after the war, because I really-- I do remember my father always learning at-- like, I woke up at night. I would hear his voice. By daytime, he was very busy because it was a big business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did your father deal with in business?

speaker: interviewee
text: With Polacks. All with Polacks, the non-Jews. But I think he dealt with some Jews too. I really-- I really wouldn't know exactly. But I think it was mostly non-Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he have a lot of people working for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We had-- first thing, we had the super with his wife. And we had workers which were working with the-- in the-- bottling the-- the beer. And we had workers which came, which would-- they came with the trucks-- came the beer, and they took off. There was few people.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So what happened in 1937? Let's start moving there.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1937, my-- it was a very dramatic thing, which my brother passed away. And this was because-- I remember him. He was so special. And after this-- and then it was-- my mother took it very hard-- of course, my father too. And they gave, like-- poured all their love to me, like they protected me extremely. In 1938, there was the-- well, it started with Hitler. And they took Poland, went-- part of Czechoslovakia, which they divided Czechoslovakia. My father was called to the army. So I presume my father must to be very young then, because he was called to the army in '38. And he went to the army, but he was, like, two days on it. There was no war. But then I remembered that we were all so frightened. I was so frightened. I was so-- that my father has to go to the war. But to me, the war was only the soldiers are fighting. I never thought that the war is any place else. And then my father was about, like, two days. And they sent him back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they send him back?

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- there wasn't any war. There was-- and, you know, Chamberlain gave away part of Czechoslovakia to Germany, and the part of Czechoslovakia Poland took. In 1939, this was-- the-- I remember 1939, end of 1939, the beginning-- end of '38, the beginning of '39, I went with my mother to Zakopane. This was-- at this time, there was-- the Olympics was in Zakopane-- the Winter Olympics. And I remember when we were going and how beautiful it was there. And, I mean, this was my best memory, which I really could remember. A wonderful time we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was just the two of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father came, like, once or twice for Shabbat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it far away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Zakopane was far away from us. It was quite a few hours by train. And I remember even which hotel we stayed. We were staying in [NON-ENGLISH], and the street was called Droga Do Bialego. I-- I had such a wonderful time there that it's remained in my memory, even the address of the hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw the Winter Olympics?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we saw the Winter Olympics, yes. And they came from all there. And there, yes-- there we did experience anti-Semitism, because I remember we took a tour to the-- to a place there in the-- in the high mountains. And we were-- it was a tour. And there were a lot of Pollacks. And they were making fun of Jews. And they were saying what to do with the Jews. You know, like we don't need them, we-- and I also heard-- you know, there is anti-Semitism-- that they say, you know, you're-- shouldn't buy by Jews. You should-- there was one senator which said, we shouldn't-- we are civilized people. We shouldn't kill Jews. But we should make a boycott for-- so they will leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: So those things I do remember-- this especially in 1939, or maybe it was before. But I remember it, like, in 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, 1939, summertime, they were talking already about the war. And August-- we went away in July, but we are a very short time. We came back. And my father got in August-- like, in middle of August, he got caught to go-- again, mobilization. And he has to go. He-- by September 15, he's supposed to be-- or 14-- I don't remember exactly. But in the middle of September, he's supposed to be in this and this place. And then, by-- completely by the end already, the last week of August, everybody was talking there will be a war and gonna be pressure of the war. I really-- to me, the war was were they gonna fight there on the front. So I was really worried about my father. What-- they gonna take him to the army and that's what they-- this was, to me, war. And then this was the last day in August, which is 1931. A lot of people were going away. They was trying to-- to go further, closer. They were going east, closer to the Russian border. And then our neighbor, which is-- was our tenant and was a good friend, this Dr. Horne-- he was a very good friend of my father, even though they were completely assimilated. They were not even as Jews. They were more like Polacks. And he was going. And then my parents said-- they asked him he should take me. And most probably he said yes, because then my parents said, you-- you're gonna go. I still remember how I was crying and begging. I felt that I'm punished because I'm not good. So my parents are sending me away, that they don't want me. I-- I-- I felt so terrible. I was crying and screaming. And then my father would force [INAUDIBLE] me on the wagon. We were going with horses-- a wagon and horses. There weren't-- we-- our town had only one car. There weren't any cars. There was one car, which one person had. So everybody went with a wagon and horses. And he put me on this wagon. And the wagon went away. And they-- like, I saw them [INAUDIBLE] was waving goodbye. And I was crying without a stop. All the way, I-- besides-- and I was frightened. Even though I knew the people very well, I was so frightened. And the most of it, I-- I felt that my parents don't love me anymore. They don't want me. They're sending me away. I mean, I really didn't know for many years-- I didn't know what the courage it took to send-- [SOBBING] to have only child [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and to send her away, not knowing what's going to be. And of course, I remember my father. He said, be good, be nice, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and remember who you are. Remember that you are a daughter of Israel. And I was screaming. We are going-- I was oblivious to everything what's going around me. I was just crying. Everything what I wanted-- I didn't care about anything. Everything what I wanted is to go home. And we were traveling. And the Germans were right behind us. You know, like, we could hear the-- the-- the fighting. We could hear everything right-- we could sometimes could hear the-- could see the fire. So we went a different place. The bombs were falling from each side. I remember we were in a town where they bombed. And we were simply stepping over dead people. Here was a hand. Here was a head. But I was so oblivious to everything that it really-- I didn't wanted to see. I didn't really care about anything. Everything what I want is to go home. And then we were traveling like this I think for about two weeks or 12 days. I don't remember exactly. Then one day, the Germans came. The Germans came. But we were with a Polish family as Polacks. And this I remember, that he came and he says, oh, the Germans are hitting the Jews now. Now it's a good time. And we were quiet. But for somehow, I really didn't care. I was happy that we are with the Germans because I'm gonna go home. And he was coming every single day and telling different stories. I mean, we tried not to go except with him-- went for shopping, whatever you need, whichever thing. There-- there wasn't any more food anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you staying, exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- I don't remember that. This was a village near a town. And I-- I don't remember exactly where it was. It was maybe not far away from [NON-ENGLISH]. I really don't remember the name of it. I-- I don't. It was a small-- we were in the village with a-- with a farmer. And he had food and-- but he was going to the city. And he was coming and telling us every single time what they do to the Jews. They were frightened. But I was-- somehow, I was oblivious. I really didn't care. It didn't bother me, anything-- everything. I said, I don't care what happened. I just want to go home to my parents. And we were like this with the Germans for about a few days. And one day, we saw passing by-- the Germans' tanks are passing by. And I remember which was written [GERMAN] which means, Stalin is a pig. How much we give him, he wants more. And then next day, we got up and the Russians were there. And we started to travel again right away. As the Russians came, we started to travel closer to the border because he had a brother in Brody, which was just on the border of the Russian. But now we're in Russia. And we were a little bit afraid, but there were a lot of people who are traveling. There were a lot of refugees which were traveling. And we came to a town, Dubna-- this I remember. And the Polacks started to fight with the-- with the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came to-- the Polack came to him and says-- to the Dr. Horne and says, you are a Polack. Why don't you go fight now? And, you know, he was, like, angry at him. But this took only, like, a few hours, this fighting. And they-- and then I was there with this family. The Russian took away their home. And we all lived in one room. Then-- then-- Now, who was-- who was with you, with Dr. Horne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Horn-- this was Dr. Horne, his wife, his-- their daughter. And they came to his brother, which had a wife and two sons. And then the-- the Russians took away his whole house. And they left only one room, where we lived all in one room. This was already, like, end of December. In the beginning of January, a cousin of mine came. And he says, I came to take-- take you home. We're gonna go home. Your parents said-- this was beginning of '40, in January. He came to Brody. And we went to-- I went with him to Lvov.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know this cousin--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: --from before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I knew him, yes. He came to visit us some. I didn't know him so well, but I knew him. He came to visit us sometimes. And we went to Lvov. We came to Lvov, found out the border's completely collapsed. We can't go anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here I was in Lvov. I didn't know what to do. And he went-- there was called-- there was like a pretzel cafeteria which all the Jewish people met. So I was sitting there, like, outside. And he went inside. He met-- and then he met there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Moishe Kenel, which was the uncle to my cousin. And he found out I'm there. He took me right away. He was a young man. He was maybe-- I don't know how old he was. But he was-- he was a young man-- not married, but he lived with a family. So he took me there. And he said, don't worry. You're gonna stay with me. And of course, I stayed with-- without a stop I was crying. I was crying day and night. And he took me. And I-- he lived by the family. And I was with his family. I started to go to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one day-- it was March-- a uncle of mine, my mother's brother, found out that I'm there. So he came for me and he took me to Sambir, to his family. But he had his wife and three sons, which one is my husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was with them there from-- I went, like, end of January till beginning of June. No, pardon me. End of March, because from January to March, I was with Kenel. And I think it was end of March or beginning of April, because it was just before Pesach. So he came and he took me to Sambir. He lived in Sambir.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was there with them till June. No. I was there. And then we heard-- this was the beginning-- end of May. We heard that the people could to go back to-- to-- to Germany-- I mean, on the German part. And I started to cry and to scream that I want to go. Maybe they're gonna let me go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came in the-- there was [NON-ENGLISH], there was the border town. And my uncle went with me, and-- and-- and cousin. And this cousin which came for me originally, he also went. And we supposed to go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came there. That must have been-- there were Ukrainians, which wanted to go-- Polacks, Ukrainians, what they wanted to go back home. There was the line, which you had to sit-- stay there for two or three days. And there was-- one day, was [INAUDIBLE] evening, I was in the line. And it was coming, night, and I was frightened. It was close to the evening. And I went over to the guard which was there and standing up front. And I start to beg him. I says, listen, I'm afraid to stay here in the line. Please let me in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it seems he got pity on me. I was frightened, small. And he let me in. And I came in before the Germans. And I start to beg them. I want to go home. I told them my parents are there. I'm lost here. I'm alone. So they asked me, Jude? I said, yes. So he gave me such a kick that I fell.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- on my knees, I still begged him, please, please let me go. So they took me and, like, they pushed me out. And I knew that I cannot go back. This was end of May. In June, one night, they came, the-- this time they were called [NON-ENGLISH]. And they came to the house. And they said, we are giving you one hour. And we are-- and you have to pack. And one of them was nice. He says, but take warm clothes, whatever you can.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're gonna have to stop the tape here. Thank you.
